coredata.dc:title: The stripping analysis of Hg(II) and Cu(II) based on hierarchical RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 composite
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yongxing
Wang, Qi
Xie, Fazhi
Xiong, Shiquan
coredata.dc:description: It is rather important to not only obtain the better detection efficiency for metal ions, but also investigate the change of sensitivity and detection limit in the simultaneous detection. Herein, a novel type of room temperature ion liquid (RTIL)/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 composite was prepared. Both the individual and simultaneous determination of Hg(II) and Cu(II) were examined by the composite electrode. The electrode exhibited the lowest limits of detection (LODs) of 0.5 and 0.3 nM for Hg(II) and Cu(II) in simultaneous determination, the highest sensitivities of 64.5 and 65.9 μA/μM, respectively, and the shortest response time. Both Hg(II) and Cu(II) films electroplated on the surface of composite were used to further analyze change of sensitivity and LOD, respectively. The thickness of films influenced the sensitivity and LOD. The proposed electrode was also performed the analysis of soil sample. The analytic method in this study could open up an avenue for the analysis and detection of metal ions, and provide a reference of electrochemical sensor based on metal films.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electroanalysis of Cu(II) and Hg(II)
Hierarchical structure
RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3
Metal film

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the monodispersed γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 composite
Electrochemical properties of the modified electrodes
Individual detection of Hg(II) and Cu(II) ions
Simultaneous detection of Hg(II) and Cu(II) ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM (a) and TEM (c) images of the γ-AlOOH micro/nanoflowers; SEM (b), TEM images (d, e) and EDS (i) of γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 micro/nanoflowers; Electron energy loss “O” (f), “Al” (g) and “Fe” (h) element mapping images of γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 micro/nanoflowers; XRD (j) patterns of the γ-AlOOH and γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 micro/nanoflowers.

Typical SWASV responses of γ-AlOOH (a), γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 (b) and RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 (c) modified electrodes in successive adding different concentrations of Hg(II) in 0.1 M NaAc-HAc solution. Insets in panels (a–c) are the corresponding calibration plots of peak current towards Hg(II) concentrations, respectively.

SWASV responses of γ-AlOOH (a), γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 (b) and RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 (c) modified electrodes towards Cu(II) in successive adding different concentrations of Cu(II) in 0.1 M NaAc-HAc solution. Insets in panels (a–c) are the corresponding linear calibration plots.

Typical SWASV response of RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 modified electrode towards Hg(II) and Cu(II) in successive adding different concentrations of Hg(II) and Cu(II) coexisting in 0.1 M NaAc-HAc solution (a). And the corresponding calibration plots of peak current towards Cu(II) (b) and Hg(II) (c) concentrations.

Typical SWASV response of γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 modified electrode towards Hg(II) and Cu(II) in successing adding different concentrations of Hg(II) and Cu(II) coexisting in 0.1 M NaAc-HAc solution (a). And the corresponding calibration plots of peak current towards Cu(II) (b) and Hg(II) (c) concentrations.

Typical SWASV response of γ-AlOOH modified electrode towards Hg(II) and Cu(II) in successing adding different concentrations of Hg(II) and Cu(II) coexisting in 0.1 M NaAc-HAc solution (a). And the corresponding calibration plots of peak current towards Cu(II) (b) and Hg(II) (c) concentrations.

Typical SWASV responses of RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 modified electrode towards Hg(II) in successive adding different concentrations of Hg(II) in 0.1 M NaAc-HAc solution containing 0.1 (a), 1 (b), 5 (c) μM Cu(II), respectively. Insets are the corresponding calibration plots.

Typical SWASVs of RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 modified electrode towards Cu(II) in successive adding different concentrations of Cu(II) in 0.1 M NaAc-HAc solution containing 0.4 (a), 0.1 (b), 2 (c) μM Cu(II), respectively. Insets are the corresponding calibration plots.
Schematic illustration of preparation and analysis towards Hg(II) and Cu(II) process of RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 hierarchical composite.
Comparison of LODs at different modified electrodes in individual and simultaneous detection.
Comparison of sensitivities at different modified electrodes in individual and simultaneous detection.
Comparison of characteristics of the RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 and other electrodes for Hg(II) and Cu(II) detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: LAP: light addressable potentiometric, IAP: Irradiated attapulgite, SPE: Screen-printed electrode, CPE: carbon paste electrode, FBT: ferrocenyl-based thioethers, NP: nanoparticle.

head.ce:title: The stripping analysis of Hg(II) and Cu(II) based on hierarchical RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 composite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Advanced Functional Composites, Huaibei Normal University, Huaibei, 23500, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Advanced Functional Composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of High Magnetic Field and Ion Beam Physical Biology, Key Laboratory of Environmental Toxicology and Pollution Control Technology of Anhui Province, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, Anhui Province, 230031, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of High Magnetic Field and Ion Beam Physical Biology
Key Laboratory of Environmental Toxicology and Pollution Control Technology of Anhui Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Anhui Jianzhu University, Hefei, 230022, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is rather important to not only obtain the better detection efficiency for metal ions, but also investigate the change of sensitivity and detection limit in the simultaneous detection. Herein, a novel type of room temperature ion liquid (RTIL)/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 composite was prepared. Both the individual and simultaneous determination of Hg(II) and Cu(II) were examined by the composite electrode. The electrode exhibited the lowest limits of detection (LODs) of 0.5 and 0.3 nM for Hg(II) and Cu(II) in simultaneous determination, the highest sensitivities of 64.5 and 65.9 μA/μM, respectively, and the shortest response time. Both Hg(II) and Cu(II) films electroplated on the surface of composite were used to further analyze change of sensitivity and LOD, respectively. The thickness of films influenced the sensitivity and LOD. The proposed electrode was also performed the analysis of soil sample. The analytic method in this study could open up an avenue for the analysis and detection of metal ions, and provide a reference of electrochemical sensor based on metal films.

ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical analysis and detection of heavy metal ions (HMIs) are important research subjects in environment area. There have been many reviews in exploiting the unique nanostructural materials with large surface, small particle diameter, ligand or good conductivity for electrochemical determination of HMIs [1–4]. Obtaining better efficiency of determination using novel materials is rather necessary, although some issues of importance in the simultaneous detection of several ions still need to be further explained in detail [5]. For example, what did resulted in changes of LOD and sensitivities in simultaneous detection, why did cause the change? Understanding these would help to develop more special and effective sensors of HMIs.

The appropriate metal elements can induce biochemical and physiological functions [6], however, excess of heavy metal such as Hg(II) and Cu(II) could harm brain, nervous and endocrine system, and initiate Menkes, Wilson, Alzheimer's and prion diseases, etc [7,8]. The World's Health Organization recommends 1 ppb and 2 ppm as a maximum permissible level of Hg(II) and Cu(II) [9,10]. So, it is rather important for the determination of Hg(II) and Cu(II) early. Various strategies such as atomic absorption spectrometry [11], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [12], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [13], colorimetry [14], Fluorimetry [15] were widely used to detect Hg(II) and Cu(II).

Electrochemical technique with a simplicity, low-cost, easy-operation, good reproducibility, is widely applied for detection of HMIs. Many synthesized materials with good characteristics were developed for determination of Hg(II) and Cu(II). Zhang [16] designed a light addressable potentiometric sensor for determination of Cu(II) and Hg(II), with limit of detection (LOD) of 0.14 and 0.1 μM (μmol L−1). Zuo [17] synthesized gold dotted s-doped carbon for simultaneously determination of Cu(II) and Hg(II) in the ranges from 0.6 to 300.9 ppb, with LOD of 0.19 ppb and 1.40 ppb. Liu [18] prepared ferrocenyl-based thioethers sensors, the obtained LOD through optical and electrochemical signals reached 0.79 and 0.97 μM for Hg(II) and Cu(II). Sahoo [19] synthesized composite of bismuth and gold particle, the LODs calculated were 0.16 ppb and 0.28 ppb for Cu(II) and Hg(II), respectively. These only reported the detection of Cu(II) and Hg(II), however, few deeply analyzed the relationship between Cu(II) and Hg(II). Inaddition, some reported about simultaneous detection of three or more metal ions. An digestion method to monitor Cd(II), Pb(II) and Cu(II) and Hg(II) was developed by Meucci [20], and the sensitivities and LODs were 11.2, 64.2, 16, 8.04 μA/μM, and 0.0026, 0.005, 0.078, 4.5 nM, respectively. Xu [21] fabricated a high active Au particle for the simultaneous detection of Cd(II), Pb(II), Cu(II), Hg(II) with a low concentration of 0.3 μM and sensitivities of 3.24, 17.63, 4.18, 3.39 μA/μM. Afkhami [22] constructed magnetite nanoparticles with a new Schiff base into carbon paste electrode for simultaneous determination of Cd(II), Cu(II) and Hg(II), the related LODs of 0.20, 0.90 and 1.00 ppb were obtained. Xiong [23] constructed the clay-ion liquid composite for electrochemical detection of Cu(II) and Hg(II) with the LODs of 2, 0.6 nM, and sensitivities of 90.4, 242.4 μA/μM. As can be seen, most of papers focused on the simultaneous detection of several HMIs, few works analyzed the individual and simultaneous detection, and further discussed the difference between them.

In the detection of HMIs, the design of an excellent electrode material is rather important. The hierarchical structure materials with various architectures, sizes, morphologies and special characteristics have been attracted in applications of catalysis, biosensors, and functional electronic devices [24–27]. Both α-AlOOH and γ-AlOOH are the two main form of bauxite ore. The γ-AlOOH is a main precursor for the production of hierarchical γ-Al2O3. Especially, the boehmite (γ-AlOOH) materials with different architecture have been widely studied due to the higher surface areas, excellent physical–chemical properties and low toxicity [28,29]. Wei [30] synthesized hierarchical AlOOH, the nanostructure was used as an efficient inorganic adsorbent. A three dimensional Al2O3 was synthesized by self-assembly of γ-AlOOH, the electrochemical results revealed the as-prepared LiMn2O4 exhibited superior stability [31]. In addition, the Fe(OH)3 and its composites showed excellent characteristics for adsorption of HMIs. For example, Mustafa [32] prepared the Fe(OH)3 particles for effectively removing Ni(II), Zn(II), Cd(II). Eral [33] investigated the determination of uranium by the adsorption of U(VI) on Fe(OH)3. Duan [34] developed a new method for the detection of metal ions by Ti(OH)4/Fe(OH)3 composite. Saha [35] designed an efficient electrochemical sensor for the detection of As(III) based on chitosan-Fe(OH)3 composite. The above mentioned Fe(OH)3 nanoparticles tended to aggregate because of high surface energy.

Thus, the development of a facile method for obtaining a mono-dispersed Fe(OH)3 particles was very important. The formed γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 hierarchical structures has higher specific surface areas and larger pore volumes through the Fe(OH)3 dispersed onto the surface of mono-dispersed γ-AlOOH [29]. However, poor conductive γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 particles could limit the sensitivity of electrochemical sensor. Room temperature ion liquid (RTIL), as an excellently conductive material, has been widely used in electrochemical areas. Incorporation of RTIL can accelerate the electron transfer, and improve the sensitivity and lower the LOD in our previous reports [23,36]. Thus, motivated by the hierarchical γ-AlOOH and Fe(OH)3 combining with metal ions, and excellent characteristic of RTIL, we would expand the properties of the γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 hierarchical composite in such a field of electrochemistry and further explore the interaction of metal ions. Especially, hierarchical property of γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 micro/nanoflowers on the electroanalytical performance of Hg(II) and Cu(II) was not investigated so far. With this background, we prepared RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 composite, based on affinity between the hydrophobic RTIL phase and γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 by electrostatic interaction and hydrophobicity [37], and examined the individual and simultaneous analysis and detection of HMIs. On the basis of determination, both Hg and Cu films were electroplated on the RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 composite electrode, and stripping behaviors of Hg(II) and Cu(II) were further analyzed. The main process of the experiment was shown in Scheme 1 .

All reagents were of an analytical grade and are commercially available from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (China) and were used without further purification. The 1-butyl-2,3-dimethylimidazolium bis-(trifluoromethanesulfon-yl) imide ([C4dmim][NTf2]) was supplied by Merck KGaA (Darmstadt, Germany).

Cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), and square wave anodic stripping voltammetry (SWASV) were performed in a conventional three-electrode system with a CHI 660D computer-controlled electrochemical workstation (CHI Instruments, Shanghai, China). A bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (diameter of 3 mm) or modified GCE served as working electrode, a platinum wire was used as a counter-electrode, and a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode as reference electrode. SWASV conditions: frequency, 15 Hz; pulse amplitude, 25 mV; potential step, 4 mV. The DPV method was conducted with a pulse amplitude of 50 mV, pulse width of 50 ms, potential step of 4 mV, and pulse period of 0.2 s.

The morphologies of the samples were observed by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Quanta 200 FEG, FEI Company, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the materials were obtained on a Philips X'pert PRO SUPER diffractometer (Netherland) with CuKα radiation. A pH meter (Mettler Toledo FE20, Switzerland) was performed the pH measurement of solution. The inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrophotometer (ICP-AES) (Thermo Fisher Scientic, model ICP 6300) was analyzed the ions of sample.

The γ-AlOOH hierarchical micro/nanoflowers were prepared based on the reported literature [29,30]. In a typical experiment, 0.52 g of sodium aluminate (NaAlO2), 0.10 g polyacrylic acid sodium salt (MW ≥ 3.0 × 107) and 4.35 g of urea (H2NCONH2) was dissolved in 20 mL of double distilled water (DDW), and stirred for 30 min, then the mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and kept at 140 °C for 10 h. Then, the obtained white precipitate was separated and washed, and dried in a vacuum at 50 °C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the monodispersed γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 composite
ce:section.ce:para: The γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 composite were prepared by hydrolysis method [29,30,32]. In brief, the γ-AlOOH flowers structure with 40 mg were added into 30 mL DDW water, and heated to boil. Successively, saturated ferric chloride solution (FeCl3) was dropped to the boiling solution, until the mixture solution became reddish brown completely. The products was washed with DDW water and ethanol, and dried in a vacuum at 60 °C for 8 h.

The preparation of RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 composite is as follows: The 10 mg mixture of γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 and ethanol solution (10 mL) containing 240 μL of RTIL was fully stirred for 12 h. Then the composite was completely separated and dried in vacuum at 50 °C for 10 h.

The 10 mg of synthesized nanomaterials were dispersed in 10 mL of DDW by sonication for 10 min, respectively, and 5 μL aliquot of dispersion were drop-coated onto the surface of GCE, respectively, and dried in air at room temperature. Prior to modification, a bare GCE was successively polished with 1, 0.3 and 0.05 mm alumina slurry, and rinsed to get a mirror-like surface, and dried under a nitrogen atmosphere.

The soil sample was treated according to our previous report [23]. In general, the soil sample with 1.0 g was placed in a muffle furnace for ashing at 600 °C for 4 h. Then, the sample was treated successively with different acids such as concentrated HCl, HNO3, HF and HClO4 in the range of 80–230 °C, respectively. Then, the obtained sample was dried in vacuum, and quantitatively transferred into a 50 mL volumetric flask for following experiments.

Fig. 1 shows the SEM images of the γ-AlOOH (Fig. 1(a)) and γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 (Fig. 1(b)) composite. It can be seen that hierarchical structure of γ-AlOOH and γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 were obtained. Fig. S1 indicates the amplified TEM characteristic of γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 hierarchical structure. It can be obvious that the effective surface area of Fe(OH)3 dispersed onto γ-AlOOH is greatly improved. The TEM morphologies of γ-AlOOH is observed in Fig. 1(c), and morphology of γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 is still flower-like, much Fe(OH)3 can be found on the surface of γ-AlOOH in Fig. (e), the amplified graph of γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 can be further seen that the Fe(OH)3 particles with very small size is successfully self-assembled on surface of the γ-AlOOH hierarchical structures in Fig. (e), which can effectively capture the target ions due to ion exchange of the groups of positively charged Fe(OH)3 etc [38,39]. The γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 composite is further analyzed using electron mapping image analysis in Fig. 1(f–h). The images indicate O-, Al-, and Fe-enriched areas of the sample, respectively. Fig. 1(i) shows the existence of O, Al and Fe elements of γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 by EDS. The crystalline characteristic of γ-AlOOH (JCPDS Card 21–1307) and γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 are confirmed by XRD pattern in the curve (1) and (2) of Fig. 1(j). The characteristic peaks of Fe(OH)3 at 2θ equal 35.4, 41.7, 50.9, 63.7 in γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 composite are observed (JCPDS Card 22–0346), suggesting the existence of Fe(OH)3 phases. So, γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 hierarchical composite is prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties of the modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical characteristics of bare, γ-AlOOH, γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 and γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3/RTIL GCEs are studied using the CV in 5 × 10−3 M (mol L−1) [Fe(CN)6]3− solution containing 0.1 M KCl shown in Fig. S2(a). The redox reaction of [Fe(CN)6]3− are observed with a potential difference (ΔE) of 82, 380 and 550 mV at the above electrodes. The cathodic and anodic current difference (ΔI) reach 122, 67 and 59 μA. It can be obversed that ΔI of γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3/RTIL/GCE is the largest, reaches 78 μA due to incorporation of RTIL. The ΔE of γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3/RTIL reaches to 390 mV and is the least among three modification electrodes. A modified Randle's equivalent circuit and the measured spectra are also shown in inset of Fig. S2(b). The impedance data are fitted with commercial software Zview2. A good fit is obtained by the depicted circuit for the experimental data. The semicircle part of EIS shows the electron-transfer resistance (RCT) at higher frequencies, while the linear segment at lower frequencies corresponds to the diffusion limited process. The RCT of γ-AlOOH, γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 and bare GCEs are 7600, 12000 and 220 Ω, respectively. However, the ΔRCT of γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3/RTIL lowers to 3950 Ω, and is significantly less than those of γ-AlOOH, γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 electrodes. In general, the electrochemical behavior of γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3/RTIL/GCE is the best at several electrodes.

The bare, γ-AlOOH, γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 and RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 modified GCEs are applied to perform the electrochemical analysis of Hg(II) containing 0.5 μM solution by SWASV, respectively, and related results are shown in Fig. S3(a). The biggest stripping current is observed at γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3/RTIL electrode, indicating the proposed electrode has the best response towards Hg(II). To further obtain the best response, SWASV, DPV and CV of γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3/RTIL electrode were employed to determine Hg(II), respectively. In cyclic voltammogram in Fig. S3(b), a reduction wave emerges at 0.24 V. The peak current for DPV appears at 0.25 V, and is larger than that of CV. The signal using SWASV is the largest among three techniques. Thus, the SWASV is employed to carry out the detection.

To further obtain the best response for electrochemical detection of Hg(II) and Cu(II) with RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3/GCE, the experimental conditions including amount of modification, pH value of supporting electrolyte (HAc-NaAc), deposition potential and time were entirely optimized in presence of 0.05 μM Cu(II) and Hg(II), and shown in Fig. S4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Individual detection of Hg(II) and Cu(II) ions
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized condition, the γ-AlOOH, γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 and RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 coated electrodes were employed to perform the individual determination of Hg(II) and Cu(II) by SWASV, respectively. The metal ions were electrodeposited on surface of the modified electrode by a negative potential, then the stripping peak was obtained using a positive potential.

The determination of Hg(II) using γ-AlOOH and γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 modified electrode are shown in Fig. 2 (a and b). The stripping potential of Hg(II) appears at about −0.23 V. The related equations in the range from 0.07 to 0.95 μM are I = −0.87 + 5.64C, I = −0.17 + 3.46C, with 80 and 15 nM of LODs, and 5.64 and 3.46 μA/μM of sensitivities, respectively. The LOD of γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 electrode reduces obviously compared to the γ-AlOOH electrode. And the sensitivity also lowers, it may be due to bad conductivity of Fe(OH)3. To RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 electrode, Fig. 2(c) indicates that the linear response relationship in the concentration range from 0.02 to 1.4 μM is I = −0.96 + 11.23C, R2 = 0.994. Both the sensitivity of 11.23 μA/μM, and the LOD of 3 nM are obtained. As can be observed, the sensitivity of RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 is about 3.2 and 2 times higher, and the LOD is about 26.7 and 5 times lower in comparison with the γ-AlOOH and γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 modified electrodes, respectively.

Fig. 3 shows the stripping behaviors of Cu at γ-AlOOH (a), γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 (b) and RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 (c) modified electrodes. The stripping peak of Cu(II) appears at about −0.04 V in Fig. 3(a–c). The linear relationships at RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3, γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 and γ-AlOOH modified electrodes from 0.006 to 1.6 μM are I(Cu) = −0.14 + 20.01C, I(Cu) = −0.26 + 3.1C, I(Cu) = −0.02 + 3.77C, respectively. The sensitivities are 20.01, 3.1, 3.77 μA/μM, and the corresponding LODs are 1, 6 and 46 nM, respectively. As can be seen, the sensitivity of RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 electrode for Cu(II) is the largest, and is about 7 and 5 folds greater than these of γ-AlOOH and γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 electrodes. The LOD is the least, and about 46 and 8 times lower than these of γ-AlOOH and γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous detection of Hg(II) and Cu(II) ions
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to further study the stripping characteristics of Hg(II) and Cu(II) ions, the simultaneous detection of Hg(II) and Cu(II) coexisting in a solution using RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3, γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3, γ-AlOOH modified electrodes are also performed by SWASV, and shown in Figs. 4–6
. The stripping potentials towards Cu(II) and Hg(II) appear at about −0.06 and 0.25 V, respectively. Fig. 4 shows that the linear equation for Hg(II) using RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 electrode in the range of 0.006–0.25 μM is I(Hg) = −1.2 + 64.5C, R2 = 0.991, with LOD of 0.5 nM. While the equation for Cu(II) in the range of 0.005–0.25 μM is I(Cu) = −1.0 + 65.9C, R2 = 0.995, the LOD calculated was 0.3 nM. The sensitivities for Hg(II) and Cu(II) are 65.9 and 64.5 μA/μM, respectively.

To γ-AlOOH modified electrode, Fig. 5 indicates that the linear equations of Cu(II) and Hg(II) in the concentration range from 0.1 to 0.4 μM are expressed as I(Hg) = −3.56 + 30.24C, I(Cu) = −5.1 + 60.4C, the sensitivities are 30.24 and 60.4 μA/μM, and the related LODs are 60 and 40 nM, respectively. As for γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 electrode in Fig. 6, the linear calibration curves for Hg(II) and Cu(II) in the range 0.03–0.28 μM, I(Hg) = −1.7 + 25C, I(Cu) = −1.3 + 38.2C, with the LODs of 8 and 5 nM, and sensitivities of 25 and 38.2 μA/μM. There are two small peaks in Fig. 6, it may be due to the redox Cu+ and Cu2+ of Cu in Hg(II) ion solution, which have no effect for detection of metal ions [40–42]. However, there is only one peak in the individual detection of Cu(II) was attributed to the effect of the RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 composite [43–45].

As can be seen, the sensitivities of RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 modified electrode for simultaneous detection of Hg(II) and Cu(II) are about 3 and 2 folds higher than these of γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3, and about 2.6 and 1 times in comparison with γ-AlOOH electrode, respectively. The LODs for Hg(II) and Cu(II) are about 6 and 17-fold lower compared with γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3, and about 120 and 133-fold lower than these of γ-AlOOH electrodes. As can be observed from the several modified electrodes, the detection efficiency of RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 electrode is the best, and both the largest sensitivity and lowest LOD are obtained from either individual or simultaneous determinations. It may be the reason that both conductive RTIL and Fe(OH)3 affinity to Hg(II) and Cu(II) ions, which improved the sensitivities and lowed the LODs. The interaction of Fe(OH)3 affinity to Hg(II) and Cu(II) that metal to surface bond was formed by replacing surface protons (H+ ions) with the metal cations [39]. On the other hand, the effective surface area of Fe(OH)3 coated on the hierarchical structure γ-AlOOH is significantly enhanced from nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm in Fig. S5, the BET surface area and Barrett–Joyner–Halenda pore volume of γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 are estimated to be 171.249 m2 g−1 and 0.400 cm3 g−1, respectively. Those of γ-AlOOH are about 145.523 m2 g−1 and 0.396 cm3 g−1, respectively. From these data, the BET specific surface area of the γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 is higher than that of the γ-AlOOH and Fe(OH)3 [29], which further resulted in the decrease of LOD. At last, γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 was only mixed with RTIL, and then used to perform the simultaneous detection of Hg(II) and Cu(II). The experimental result was shown in Fig. S6. It indicated that sensitivity and LOD of mixture electrode were obviously less than those of RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3, which indicated the form of RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 composite.

To further analyze the best behavior of RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3, the dynamic characteristics of potential-time response at γ-AlOOH, γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3, RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 electrodes are investigated in 0.1 μM of Cu(II) and Hg(II) existing in a solution, and the related results are shown in Fig. S7. As can be seen that the response time of RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 composite electrode is about 4 s, these of γ-AlOOH and γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 electrodes are about 10 s and 21 s, respectively. The response time of RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 is the shortest due to import of RTIL with good conductivity, and larger surface area of γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3, both γ-AlOOH and Fe(OH)3 with bad conductivity of could stretch the response time of modified electrode. Thus, the RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 electrochemical sensor can be used for detection of Cu(II) and Hg(II) in the least time compared with others.

To further compared with the sensitivities and LOD of RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 towards Cu(II) and Hg(II) in individual and simultaneous detection, the related results are further analyzed in Tables 1 and 2
. It can be observed that the sensitivities of the RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 in simultaneous detection are the largest, and 3.2 and 5.7 times higher than those of individual detection. LOD is the lowest, and 3.3 and 4 folds lower than those of the individual. The same situation can be obtained at γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 and γ-AlOOH modified electrodes. Through further comparison of the analytical results at three kinds of modified electrodes, the same phenomenon is found that the LODs of Hg(II) and Cu(II) in simultaneous detection obviously descend, and the sensitivities rise significantly compared with the individual.

In order to study the possible reason that both sensitivities and LODs of simultaneous detection are better than these of the individual, it was thought that Hg or Cu film deposited on the composite electrode caused the change. So, either Hg or Cu film is electrodeposited on the surface of RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 composite, and further analyze the stripping behaviors of Cu and Hg, respectively.

Firstly, several concentrations of Cu(II) containing 0.1, 1 and 5 μM were fixed in HAc-NaAc buffer solution, respectively. Then, Hg(II) solution were added successively, the stripping characteristic of Hg were analyzed at RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 plated by Cu film, respectively. At the beginning, 0.1 μM Cu(II) was electrodeposited on the composite electrode, the stripping signal of Hg after a 120 s deposition time increases with increasing of Hg(II) solution in Fig. 7 (a). The linear equation of Hg(II) from 0.075 to 0.35 μM is I(Hg) = −2.1 + 23.63C. When Cu(II) with 1 μM is fixed in buffer, the calibration curve of Hg(II) in Fig. 7(b) from 0.05 to 0.45 μM is I(Hg) = −1.85 + 35.39C. As can be seen, the sensitivity of Hg at Cu/RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 electrode improves, and corresponding LOD reduces. So, the detection effect of Hg increases. While Cu(II) solution increases to 5 μM, the curve equation in the range of 0.2–3 μM is I(Hg) = −3.25 + 17.27C. The detection efficiency of Hg(II) reduces. It can be observed that the corresponding sensitivities for Hg(II) are 23.63, 35.39 and 17.27 μA/μM when Cu(II) concentration are 0.1, 1 and 5 μM, respectively. And the corresponding LOD of Hg are 10, 3 and 35 nM, which indicates that sensitivities and LODs are effected by Cu(II) concentration. The sensitivity of the electrode towards Hg(II) is the largest, and the LOD is the lowest after RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 is electrodeposited by 1 μM Cu(II) solution. In addition, the stripping peaks of Cu are always unchanged, even the signal of Hg(II) increases successively; it indicates that the Cu film electrode based on hierarchical structure could be developed for the detection of metal ion (see Fig. 7).

Under the similar condition, the effect of Hg film was electrodeposited on the surface of RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 electrode, the stripping behaviors of Cu(II) in Fig. 8 were analyzed by fixing Hg(II) solution containing 0.1, 0.4, 2 μM in HAc-NaAc solution, respectively. The calibration curves of Cu(II) ranged from 0.01 to 0.35 μM are I(0.1 μM) = −0.55 + 39.64C, I(0.4 μM) = −0.77 + 24.74C and I(2 μM) = −1.33 + 30.8C, the sensitivities are 24.74, 39.64 and 30.8 μA/μM, and the LODs are 8, 0.8 and 20 nM, respectively. It can be observed that when the concentration of Hg(II) is 0.4 μM fixed in the supporting electrolyte, the sensitivity of Hg film coated RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 towards Cu is the largest, and LOD is the lowest. However, the excess of Hg film could cause the low of sensitivity and rise of LOD. So, it is supposed that the difference between simultaneous and individual detection of Hg(II) and Cu(II) are related with the film of Cu or Hg plated on the surface of electrode. The appropriate thickness of film could further improve the sensitivity and reduce the LOD. It also indicates that Hg or Cu films electrode based on RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 can be developed to carry out the determination of HMIs. Although, the sensitivity towards Hg(II) or Cu(II) through forming Cu or Hg film at the RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 modified electrode are still lower, and LOD are higher than these of simultaneous detection of Hg(II) and Cu(II) coexisting in a solution shown in Table S1, it may be reason that Hg and Cu films formed could take up the partial active sites of RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 surface. However, the sensitivities and LOD are better than these of the individual detection, related study is further analyzing.

In the experiments above mentioned, all of the same modified electrodes are repeated five times, and the resultant repeatability as relative standard deviations (RSDs) are all less than 3.9%, showing good repeatability of the modified electrodes.
The proposed electrode was placed for 24 h under room temperature condition, showed good stability calculated with RSDs of less than 3.3%.

To further understand behaviors of the RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 electrode, characteristics of the proposed electrode with previously reported electrodes for determination of Hg(II) and Cu(II) were displayed in Table 3 . It can be observed that the proposed modified electrode has lower LOD, and higher sensitivity than most others electrodes. The excellent sensitivity of the sensor benefited from the improvement on RTIL, in addition, the larger surface area of γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 hierarchical structure toward Hg(II) and Cu(II) improved the reactive-sites of Fe(OH)3, and lowered the LOD.

An interference analysis of any possible effects caused by the presence of other ions in the electrochemical determination of Cu(II) and Hg(II) at RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 electrode was discussed. The different metal ions such as Cd(II), Ni(II), Zn(II), K(I), Ca(II), Na(I), Fe(III), Pb(II), Cr(III), Cr(VI), Mn(II), Bi(III) and Ag(I), were analyzed. No interference was found when the molar ratio was 1: 65. It affirmed that the as-prepared electrode was a better electrochemical sensor for analysis of Cu(II) and Hg(II).

To examine the real application of the RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 electrode, three soil samples are collected and treated. The simultaneous determination of Cu(II) and Hg(II) in soil sample is performed using the standard addition method. Results were compared with those obtained by ICP-AES shown in Table S2. The experiments indicate that the proposed electrode is accurate, and can be used for detection of real sample. The experiments were repeated five times with good reproducibility as the obtained relative standard deviations (RSDs) were all less than 3.5%.

We have designed and prepared RTIL/γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 hierarchical structure composite. The as-prepared electrode exhibited the lowest detection limit, good selectivity, and the highest sensitivity towards Cu(II) and Hg(II) compared with other electrodes. The excellent electrochemical properties might ascribe the special porous morphology of γ-AlOOH/Fe(OH)3 hierarchical structure, as well as the good conductivity ability of RTIL. The efficiency of simultaneous detection was better than these of the individual. It was expected that the research of Cu and Hg films could also offer a potential application for detection of metal ions. The sensor with satisfactory results was employed to determine Cu(II) and Hg(II) in real sample. The electrochemical method further provided a reference for analysis and interaction of heavy metal ions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province, China (No. 1808085MB37, 1608085MB43 and 1708085ME96) and HIPS(Y49LH55893), Chinese Academy of Science (CAS), China. This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (No.21777001 and 51573001).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2018.08.020.
